note: Mathlib/RingTheory/LaurentSeries.lean:102 — `abbrev LaurentSeries (R) [Zero R] := R⟦ℤ⟧`, docstring: "LaurentSeries R is the type of formal Laurent series with coefficients in R, denoted R⸨X⸩"; this is the concept's direct definition.
verify_note: Confirmed against mathlib4 source: the abbrev + docstring literally define the same object Wikidata Q425432/enwiki 'Laurent series' describes (power series generalization allowing negative exponents); decl existence confirmed in declaration-data.json.

note: Mathlib/Analysis/SpecificLimits/Normed.lean:776 — docstring literally "The alternating series test" for antitone sequences tending to 0; this theorem (Leibniz test) is the central content of the Wikipedia article on alternating series.
verify_note: Overclaimed match_kind: decl is a convergence *theorem* for one instantiation (antitone f, tends to 0) of the alternating series test, not a definition of the concept 'alternating series' (an infinite series ∑(-1)^n a_n) — Mathlib has no AlternatingSeries def at all (grep confirms), and en.wikipedia.org/Alternating_series treats the test as one subsection among several (examples, abs/conditional convergence, rearrangement), not the whole concept; 'related' would be honest, 'exact' is not — the decl does not BE the concept.

note: Mathlib/NumberTheory/GaussSum.lean:72 — `def gaussSum (χ : MulChar R R') (ψ : AddChar R R') : R' := ∑ a, χ a * ψ a`; Gauss sums are a classical named instance of exponential sums (character sums), not the general concept itself — no general exponential-sum definition exists in Mathlib.
verify_note: Confirmed: gaussSum def matches the note (∑ a, χ a * ψ a), decl exists in oracle; match_kind special_case + low confidence is honestly calibrated (Gauss sum is a genuine special case of exponential/character sums per enwiki Q4446020, no overclaim of exact).
